trajectory_summary: The call is a procedural win — bill captured, appointment booked — but an emotional loss. Hiram handed the rep his bill-pain story three times and received no flip, no alignment-against-utility, and no curiosity creation in return. The appointment was booked on 'no commitment, just informational' framing, which is structurally fragile: Hiram is explicitly price-shopping with a competitor already scheduled, and his motivation to show up is low because the rep never made him feel the cost of staying on ComEd.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is unknown, but the appointment should be flagged as conditional/fragile. Hiram explicitly stated he won't commit until he sees the best price, has a competitor visit already scheduled, and was booked on 'no commitment' framing. This appointment carries meaningful no-show risk.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Bill-pain anchor and flip — three missed opportunities
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Oh, wow. Yeah. That's a huge jump.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Hiram handed the rep his bill-pain story three times across the call (02:17, 09:53, 11:29) and received a brief acknowledgment each time before the rep returned to logistics. The codex names this exact pattern — 'capture-and-drop' — as the universal floor failure. The rep has the emotional raw material; she lacks the habit of stopping to use it. The manager should play all three moments back-to-back and ask the rep what she heard each time, then work on a short bridge phrase she can deploy without breaking the navigation thread.

key_moments.quote: But after I put that that you know, I after I changed, but they went up to about, you know, 200 and the first one came out to 209, and this one came out to 224.
key_moments.stewart_read: Hiram has just disclosed that going all-electric tripled his bill — from $79 to $209-$224. This is not a complaint; it is a qualification signal. A customer whose bill tripled because they converted every appliance to electric is exactly the high-usage profile solar is built for. The rep's 'Oh, wow. Yeah. That's a huge jump' acknowledges the number but drops the emotional thread immediately. Strategic read: the rep may have been holding the flip for after bill capture. Reactive read: the rep had no flip ready and defaulted to logistics. The reactive read holds — the flip never came, not even after the bill was captured.

key_moments.quote: Because it jumped from 60 to $2.40 because, you know, the last the last two months, I have the new the new system.
key_moments.stewart_read: Second unprompted bill-pain disclosure. Hiram is connecting his new all-electric system to the bill spike — he understands the cause and is telling the rep. The rep responds 'Okay. Gotcha. That's good to know.' and pivots to the cost-vs-usage display problem. This is the 'capture-and-drop' failure the codex names explicitly. The rep has now missed the flip twice.

key_moments.quote: for the 02/2025, my average was $66.39. Okay? I think it was that was monthly. Okay? So now from that, it jumped to $2.29 the last two, you know, the last last month.
key_moments.stewart_read: Third unprompted bill-pain disclosure. Hiram is now providing historical context — he knows his pre-electric average ($66) and his post-electric bills ($229). He is doing the rep's emotional work for her. The rep responds 'Okay.' and immediately asks 'Are you guys seeing the usage I was talking about?' The pattern is now three-for-three: capture, acknowledge minimally, return to logistics.

key_moments.quote: I got the program. I went electric. So I don't have I don't have no gas. What I got is electrical. Everything's electrical. I got the, the, the stove, the dryer. I got the system from the AC and the furnace together.
key_moments.stewart_read: Hiram is describing a fully electrified home — stove, dryer, AC, and furnace all on electric. This is a high-load profile that makes him an above-average solar candidate. The rep does not acknowledge the load profile as a qualification signal; she stays focused on the bill-capture task. This is not a failure of procedure but a missed opportunity to build curiosity: a fully electric home with a tripled bill is a stronger solar candidate than a partial-electric home.
